title: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds point-by-point to Scott Klusendorf's Gospel Coalition article defending pro-life incrementalism, exposing rhetorical demonization, mischaracterizations of Bill Ascol, and a string of broken analogies (wife-beating governors, prisoner-of-war marches, IVF) while defending immediatism, nullification, and the abolitionist refusal to sign or support pro-life regulations.
audiences: Reformed pastors and seminary readers of The Gospel Coalition
state legislators weighing abolition vs. regulation bills
Christians wrestling with civil authority and nullification

questions.question: How does Klusendorf rhetorically frame abolitionists in his Gospel Coalition opening, and why does Silberman call it 'garbage argumentation'?
questions.answer: Klusendorf opens by depicting abolitionists as predators with knives hunting pro-lifers to gut their legislative work. Silberman treats this as cheap rhetorical demonizing rather than substantive engagement, noting that ideological arguments can at least be answered, but smear language like this cannot.
questions.quote: Now that the Supreme Court has let stand (for now) a Texas pro-life law that prohibits abortions after fetal heartbeat, the knives are out for pro-lifers, and not just from pro-abortionists. Abolitionists are also in the hunt to gut pro-life legislative efforts.

questions.question: What does Silberman say Klusendorf misrepresents about Bill Ascol's call for church discipline?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and seminary readers of The Gospel Coalition
questions.answer: Klusendorf claims Ascol called for disfellowshipping politicians who don't vote along abolitionist lines, but Ascol actually limited his call to politicians who vote against a bill that would abolish abortion outright. Silberman frames this as a deliberate, repeated sleight-of-hand because the honest version would not win Klusendorf's argument.
questions.quote: He didn’t. He suggested church discipline for those who *vote against a bill to abolish abortion*. But Klusendorf can’t say that. Obviously, he can’t win the argument if he portrays Bill accurately. So he doesn’t. He lies about Bill repeatedly, even after being publicly corrected.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say current pro-life leaders fit Klusendorf's 'Bob' character even more than 'Abraham' in the wife-beating governor analogy?
questions.audience: state legislators weighing abolition vs. regulation bills
questions.answer: Klusendorf's Abraham signs incremental bills while pushing toward total protection, but Silberman observes that real pro-life leaders sign incremental bills and then refuse to enforce them, or even market them as moderate compromises that preserve abortion access. Governor Stitt's 'we'll let the courts work it out' and Governor Abbott's framing of the heartbeat bill as giving women 'at least six weeks' are presented as evidence.
questions.quote: They sign incremental bills and promise not to enforce them. (As he signed a slew of pro-life bills Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt told the media, “we’ll let the courts work out if any of those get overturned.”) They sign incremental bills and promote them as moderate compromises that still allow for abortion.

questions.question: Do abolitionists support repealing existing pro-life regulations?
questions.answer: Yes, but only inside an abolition bill that establishes a just standard by treating preborn children as persons under the homicide code. Abolitionists oppose stand-alone repeals like the New York Reproductive Health Act because such bills create an unjust standard rather than abolishing abortion.
questions.quote: Abolitionists support the repeal of pro-life regulations, but only in the context of an abolition bill which is abolishing abortion. Abolitionists only support bills which establish a just standard, according to God’s definition of justice.

questions.question: How do abolitionists answer the claim that pro-life regulations have meaningfully reduced abortion numbers?
questions.answer: Silberman lists four standing answers: any reduction does not erase the inherent injustice of regulating murder, regulations delay abolition by substituting for it, they delay abolition by dehumanizing the unprotected unborn, and the claimed reductions are usually exaggerated. He insists abolitionists have made these points repeatedly across many media.
questions.quote:
We have repeatedly made clear in articles, podcasts, pamphlets, conference presentations, and more that any alleged reduction in the abortion rate as a result of a pro-life regulation:

1. [Does not change the inherent injustice](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t1zMoSxevkg) of regulating abortion
2. [Delays the abolition of abortion](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4T-7szob5pA&t=3365s) by serving as a substitute for abolition, causing more deaths in the long run

questions.question: Would abolitionists support a bill that bans abortion but does not address IVF deaths?
questions.audience: state legislators weighing abolition vs. regulation bills
questions.answer: Yes, because abolition bills function by establishing personhood at fertilization and removing the abortion exception in the homicide code. Just as homicide statutes do not enumerate stabbing, shooting, or strangulation separately, abolition bills do not need to enumerate IVF, surgical, or chemical killing. The only IVF-related bill abolitionists would oppose is one that writes an explicit fertility-clinic exception into law.
questions.quote: Abolition bills do not directly address each form of murder. All they do is establish that life begins at fertilization and remove the exception in the homicide code that allows for abortion. Upon the passage of such a bill, preborn children would be treated by law the same as everyone else.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say the prisoner-of-war 'death march' analogy fails as a defense of incremental legislation?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and seminary readers of The Gospel Coalition
questions.answer: The captured officer is not a lawmaker; he is a combatant whose duty is to win a war while preserving as many of his men as possible. Immediatism, by contrast, is a socio-political principle rooted in repentance, and incremental abortion bills fail to call the state to repentance because they refuse to present the righteous standard.
questions.quote: The socio-political principle of immediatism does not apply to war because immediatism is about repentance. In the context of a culture that murders its children, repentance is the key. For repentance to occur, violators of God’s law need to be called to repent. For there to be a true call to repentance, a righteous standard has to be presented.

questions.question: What is the abolitionist response to Klusendorf's claim that pro-lifers lack the legal authority to abolish abortion?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with civil authority and nullification
questions.answer: Silberman argues Klusendorf is treating the Supreme Court as the ultimate source of legal authority, when in fact God's Word, the U.S. Constitution's Equal Protection Clause, and state constitutions like Oklahoma's already authorize and require the abolition of abortion. State officials therefore have both biblical and constitutional duty to act, regardless of SCOTUS.
questions.quote: In order to say this, Klusendorf must hold a view of legal authority which views the supreme court as the ultimate authority unto whom unconditional submission is due. He does not have a proper view of the hierarchy of authority, believing that there is no legal authority but that which is derived from SCOTUS.

questions.question: How does Silberman use the Nuremberg trials to answer the 'just following orders' defense of pro-life incrementalism?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with civil authority and nullification
questions.answer: He cites Justice Robert H. Jackson's prosecution of Nazi defendants, where neither 'superior orders' nor 'acts of state' could excuse participation in mass murder. Applied to abortion, a state-level official cannot hide behind Supreme Court rulings to evade his obligation to outlaw the killing of preborn humans, because both SCOTUS and state officials are 'under God and the law.'
questions.quote: Applying this to the present situation, a State-level official cannot shirk his duty to uphold justice and outlaw murder by appealing to orders from the supreme court. Both the supreme court and State-level officials are “under God and the law.”

questions.question: How does Silberman distinguish nullification from secession against Doug Wilson and Klusendorf?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with civil authority and nullification
questions.answer: Wilson claims that being a true abolitionist requires secession; Klusendorf softens this to nullification being 'akin to' secession. Silberman defines nullification as upholding higher law in defiance of a superior while remaining in the union, whereas secession declares full independence. He cites Jefferson Davis, an opponent of nullification, conceding that the two are 'antagonistic principles.'
questions.quote: To nullify is to uphold higher law in defiance of an order from a superior while not acting to separate from or remove the authority. To secede is to declare complete independence from the authority.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the 'And then what?' rhetorical question used by Van Maren and Klusendorf?
questions.answer: Abolitionists do not need a guaranteed forecast of every consequence because their duty is fixed and the results belong to God. Federal funding threats or troop deployments are possible but historically rare, and even if they occurred, the obligation to expose tyrants rather than appease them does not change.
questions.quote: Abolitionists do not purport to know exactly how abolition and nullification would play out, and we don’t need to. Duty is ours, results belong to God.

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe nullification actually increases the chance Roe gets overturned?
questions.answer: He frames justices like Roberts and Kavanaugh as politically reactive 'leaves in the wind' who responded to left-wing pressure on Obamacare and would respond to abolitionist pressure on Roe. State-level defiance, abolitionist amicus briefs, and unseating incrementalists in legislatures collectively raise the political cost of preserving Roe, making reversal more likely than continued appeasement has ever made it.
questions.quote: It is conceivable that *Roe* would be overturned in *Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health* because of the rise of the Abolitionist Movement. Men like John Roberts and Brett Kavanuagh are leaves in the wind, always seeking to be in the center of wherever the political winds are blowing.

questions.question: What does Silberman point to as the crowning example of pro-life movement self-deception?
questions.answer: He cites Focus on the Family VP Gary Bauer claiming victory after Planned Parenthood v. Casey, a decision in which an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority upheld and even strengthened Roe. Bauer celebrated because some pro-life regulations survived, which Silberman calls a pathetic posture toward a crushing defeat.
questions.quote: An 8-1 Republican-appointed majority (six of whom had been appointed by pro-life Republicans in the previous 19 years to replace six pro-*Roe* justices) upheld and even strengthened *Roe*. But because a few of the pro-life regulations in question were allowed, Bauer and other pro-lifers claimed victory.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist scriptural argument is the decisive battleground rather than pragmatics?
questions.audience: Reformed pastors and seminary readers of The Gospel Coalition
questions.answer: Even if every pragmatic calculation favored Klusendorf, scripture alone would still settle the case for abolitionism. Silberman observes that attacks on the Southern Baptist Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, including from seminary professors, have largely avoided scriptural argument because incrementalists cannot 'out-exegete' abolitionists.
questions.quote: If Klusendorf had every pragmatic calculation on his side, and all abolitionists had was scripture, we would be right and he would be wrong.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Earlier installment in the same Free the States series critiquing Scott Klusendorf's incrementalist apologetics.

related.title: Responding to Klusendorf and Newman: Trading a Prophetic Voice for Cowardly Compromise
related.reason: Direct predecessor article that Silberman references when noting Klusendorf's repeated mischaracterization of Bill Ascol.

related.title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
related.reason: Continues the critique of Doug Wilson as an incrementalist thought leader whose talking points appear in Klusendorf's article.

related.title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
related.reason: Another point-by-point rebuttal of a Reformed incrementalist arguing in Wilson's orbit.

related.title: Are Nullification’s Advocates the “Villains” of American History?
related.reason: Develops the historical case for nullification that Silberman defends against Klusendorf and Wilson's secession charge.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Concise framing of the immediatism principle Silberman invokes when dismantling the prisoner-of-war analogy.

related.title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
related.reason: Follows up on Silberman's prediction here that the abolitionist movement could pressure SCOTUS toward overturning Roe in Dobbs.
